era_context: Continuation of the early 2nd century BCE Hellenistic world. The Seleucid Empire remains internally divided; Ptolemaic Egypt maintains relative stability. Rome is consolidating its Mediterranean dominance and beginning to intervene in Eastern affairs. The Han Dynasty under Emperor Wen continues its period of economic and administrative consolidation. This is a transitional period before major conflicts reshape the Eastern Mediterranean.

events.title: Antiochus III the Great begins military campaigns to restore Seleucid power
events.description: Antiochus III (r. 223–187 BCE), known as 'the Great,' initiates a series of military campaigns to restore Seleucid control over territories lost during the civil war with his brother Antiochus Hierax and to reassert dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean. These campaigns, collectively known as the 'Anabasis' or 'Great Journey,' will extend over the next decade and take him from Syria to the borders of India. In 193 BCE, he is in the early stages of these campaigns, likely focusing on Anatolia and northern Syria.
events.certainty_note: Antiochus III's campaigns are well-documented in outline, but the precise chronology of individual campaigns in 193 BCE is uncertain. Polybius provides the most detailed account, but he wrote over a century later.

events.title: Roman Senate debates Eastern Mediterranean policy
events.description: As Antiochus III's power grows and Seleucid ambitions expand, the Roman Senate becomes increasingly concerned about Eastern Mediterranean stability. While no major military action occurs in 193 BCE, Roman diplomatic and political attention is turning toward the East. This period marks the beginning of Rome's active engagement in Hellenistic affairs, which will culminate in the Syrian War (192–188 BCE).
events.certainty_note: Roman concern about Antiochus III is well-documented, but the precise timing of Senate debates in 193 BCE is not explicitly attested. This entry reflects the broader political context.

events.title: Han Dynasty: Continued economic expansion and trade
events.description: Under Emperor Wen, the Han Dynasty continues its period of economic growth. Agricultural productivity increases due to reduced taxation and improved irrigation. Trade networks expand, including early connections with Central Asia and the Xiongnu peoples to the north. This period of prosperity strengthens the Han state and allows for cultural and technological advancement.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Han economic expansion under Emperor Wen is well-documented. Specific events in 193 BCE are not individually attested.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating and significance of Antiochus III's early campaigns are debated among scholars. Some sources suggest his major campaigns began slightly later (around 212 BCE for the Anabasis proper), while others place early campaigns in the 190s BCE. The extent to which Rome was actively engaged in Eastern affairs in 193 BCE versus simply monitoring the situation is also contested. Some scholars argue that Roman intervention was reactive rather than proactive.
historiographic_note: Sources for 193 BCE are similar to 194 BCE: primarily Hellenistic and Chinese. Polybius remains the most detailed source for Seleucid and Roman affairs, but he wrote over a century later. Chinese sources are more contemporary in spirit but compiled centuries after events. The record is heavily weighted toward political and military history; economic and social developments are underrepresented except in Chinese sources. Western-centric bias is evident in the surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Antiochus III's campaigns escalated tensions leading to Syrian War
Roman political concern led to military intervention
